chunk_summary: This section discusses phenotype-based evaluation and red flags for secondary causes of growth faltering in pediatric nutrition, emphasizing differential diagnoses and resource-adapted work-ups for non-responding cases.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of phenotype in identifying primary versus secondary causes of growth faltering.
Recognize red flags such as bilateral pitting edema, severe pallor, and chronic symptoms in pediatric malnutrition.
Learn resource-adapted diagnostic approaches for secondary causes of growth faltering.
Identify when to document non-response and pursue secondary etiologies instead of escalating caloric intake.
key_facts: Wasting indicates acute intake deficit or recent illness, while stunting suggests chronic deprivation or repeated infections.
Bilateral pitting edema may indicate kwashiorkor but can mimic nephrotic syndrome, cardiac failure, hepatic disease, or severe anemia with fluid overload.
Severe pallor requires evaluation beyond iron deficiency, including hemoglobinopathies, chronic infections, malaria, and parasitic diseases.
Minimum work-up includes CBC with indices, random glucose in sick wasted children, and urine dipstick in cases of edema.
Non-response to nutritional interventions should prompt investigation of secondary causes rather than escalating caloric intake.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Bilateral pitting edema is a triage diagnosis but also a differential diagnosis. Nutritional edema (kwashiorkor) is common in severe malnutrition, yet nephrotic syndrome, cardiac failure, hepatic disease, and severe anemia with fluid overload can mimic it.
document_key_topics: Red flags for secondary causes of growth faltering
